input_row.title: Ensure that an exclusionary device code flow policy is considered
input_row.description: Conditional Access Policies can be used to prevent the Device code authentication flow. Device code flow should be permitted only for users that regularly perform duties that explicitly require the use of Device Code to authenticate, such as utilizing Azure with PowerShell.
input_row.rationale: Attackers use Device code flow in phishing attacks and, if successful, results in the attacker gaining access tokens and refresh tokens which are scoped to "user_impersonation", which can perform any action the user has permission to perform. Impact: Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2 is required. This policy should be tested using the Report-only mode before implementation. Without a full and careful understanding of the accounts and personnel who require Device code authentication flow, implementing this policy can block authentication for users and devices who rely on Device code flow. For users and devices that rely on device code flow authentication, more secure alternatives should be implemented wherever possible.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. From Azure Home open the Portal menu in the top left and select Microsoft Entra ID. 2. Scroll down in the menu on the left and select Security. 3. Select on the left side Conditional Access. 4. Select Policies. 5. Select the policy you wish to audit, then: o Under Assignments > Users, review the users and groups for the personnel the policy will apply to o Under Assignments > Target resources, review the cloud apps or actions for the systems the policy will apply to o Under Conditions > Authentication Flows, review the configuration to ensure Device code flow is selected o Under Access Controls > Grant - Confirm that Block access is selected.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal Part 1 of 2 - Create the policy and enable it in Report-only mode. 1. From Azure Home open the portal menu in the top left and select Microsoft Entra ID. 2. Scroll down in the menu on the left and select Security. 3. Select on the left side Conditional Access. 4. Select Policies. 5. Click the + New policy button, then: 6. Provide a name for the policy. 7. Under Assignments, select Users then: o Under Include, select All users o Under Exclude, check Users and groups and only select emergency access accounts 8. Under Assignments, select Target resources then: o Under Include, select All cloud apps o Leave Exclude blank unless you have a well defined exception 9. Under Conditions > Authentication Flows, set Configure to Yes then: o Select Device code flow o Select Done 10. Under Access Controls > Grant, select Block Access. 11. Set Enable policy to Report-only. 12. Click Create. Allow some time to pass to ensure the sign-in logs capture relevant conditional access events. These events will need to be reviewed to determine if additional considerations are necessary for your organization (e.g. many legitimate use cases of device code authentication are observed). NOTE: The policy is not yet 'live,' since Report-only is being used to audit the effect of the policy. Part 2 of 2 - Confirm that the policy is not blocking access that should be granted, then toggle to On. 1. With your policy now in report-only mode, return to the Microsoft Entra blade and click on Sign-in logs. 2. Review the recent sign-in events - click an event then review the event details (specifically the Report-only tab) to ensure: o The sign-in event you're reviewing occurred after turning on the policy in report-only mode o The policy name from step 6 above is listed in the Policy Name column o The Result column for the new policy shows that the policy was Not applied (indicating the device code authentication flow was not blocked) 3. If the above conditions are present, navigate back to the policy name in Conditional Access and open it. 4. Toggle the policy from Report-only to On. 5. Click Save. Default Value: This policy does not exist by default. References: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/concept- authentication-flows#device-code-flow 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-identity- management#im-7-restrict-resource-access-based-on--conditions 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional- access/concept-conditional-access-report-only 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/how-to-policy- authentication-flows Additional Information: These policies should be tested by using the What If tool in the References. Setting these can and will create issues with logging in for users until they use an MFA device linked to their accounts. Further testing can also be done via the insights and reporting resource in References which monitors Azure sign ins.
input_row.impact: Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2 is required. This policy should be tested using the Report-only mode before implementation. Without a full and careful understanding of the accounts and personnel who require Device code authentication flow, implementing this policy can block authentication for users and devices who rely on Device code flow. For users and devices that rely on device code flow authentication, more secure alternatives should be implemented wherever possible.
input_row.reference: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/concept- authentication-flows#device-code-flow 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-identity- management#im-7-restrict-resource-access-based-on--conditions 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional- access/concept-conditional-access-report-only 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/how-to-policy- authentication-flows Additional Information: These policies should be tested by using the What If tool in the References. Setting these can and will create issues with logging in for users until they use an MFA device linked to their accounts. Further testing can also be done via the insights and reporting resource in References which monitors Azure sign ins.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Accessing the Azure Portal:**
   - Navigate to the Azure Home and open the portal menu in the top left. Select "Microsoft Entra ID."

2. **Reviewing Conditional Access Policies:**
   - Scroll down in the menu on the left and select "Security."
   - Click on "Conditional Access."

3. **Examining Policy Details:**
   - Select "Policies."
   - Identify the policy you wish to audit and click on it.
   
4. **Checking Assignments:**
   - Under "Assignments > Users," validate the users and groups that the policy applies to, ensuring it includes only those who require device code authentication.
   - Under "Assignments > Target resources," review the list of cloud apps or actions affected by the policy, confirming they are appropriate.

5. **Verifying Authentication Flow:**
   - In "Conditions > Authentication Flows," check that Device code flow is selected. 

6. **Access Control Review:**
   - Under "Access Controls > Grant," confirm that "Block Access" is selected.

7. **Testing the Policy:**
   - Ensure the policy is enabled in "Report-only" mode for evaluation of its potential impacts. 
   - After a reasonable time for logging (make sure sign-in logs capture relevant conditional access events), return to the "Sign-in logs" under the Microsoft Entra blade.

8. **Log Analysis:**
   - Review recent sign-in events. Click on an event and check the details in the "Report-only" tab to ensure that:
     - The policy name appears in the "Policy Name" column.
     - The "Result" column shows that the policy was "Not applied," indicating the device code authentication flow was not blocked.

9. **Final Adjustment:**
   - If the conditions met above confirm no legitimate use cases are being impacted, toggle the policy from Report-only to On.
   - Click "Save" to apply the changes to the policy.

10. **Document Your Findings:**
    - Keep a record of your review process and decisions made based on log analysis to track compliance and changes in access needs in the future.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- The classification of this audit approach as Manual is partially correct. While manual verification through the Azure Portal is a valid method, this process can also be automated using Azure APIs, Azure CLI, or PowerShell. Azure provides capabilities to manage and review Conditional Access Policies programmatically. For instance, the Azure AD PowerShell module or Microsoft Graph API can be utilized to list and evaluate Conditional Access Policies, including checking assignments and conditions related to device code authentication flows.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- The manual steps provided are generally accurate and cover the necessary aspects of reviewing Conditional Access Policies related to device code flow. However, there are some areas where the process could be streamlined or enhanced with automation. Additionally, the naming convention for the program is not mentioned, which should follow a standard format like `azure_conditionalaccess_policy_review`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation Opportunities:** Utilize Azure CLI or PowerShell scripts to automate the retrieval and evaluation of Conditional Access Policies. For example, using the `Get-AzureADMSConditionalAccessPolicy` cmdlet in PowerShell can help automate the extraction of policy details.
  
- **Missing Considerations:** The assessment does not mention the use of Microsoft Graph API, which is a powerful tool for interacting with Azure AD resources, including Conditional Access Policies. Incorporating this API could enhance the audit process by allowing for more detailed and automated checks.

- **Alternative Approach Suggestions:** Implement a script using Microsoft Graph API to programmatically check for policies that include device code flow and verify their assignments and conditions. This would reduce the need for manual intervention and increase accuracy and efficiency.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the manual steps are generally accurate, the lack of automation consideration and potential for human error in manual processes reduces confidence. Incorporating automated checks would increase reliability and repeatability.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Modify the approach** to include automated checks using Azure CLI, PowerShell, or Microsoft Graph API. This will enhance the efficiency and accuracy of the audit process. Additionally, ensure the naming convention for any scripts or programs follows a standardized format for clarity and consistency.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The task of auditing Conditional Access Policies, including the verification of assignments and conditions related to device code authentication flows, can be automated using Azure's capabilities. Azure provides APIs, such as Microsoft Graph API, and PowerShell modules that allow for programmatic access to Conditional Access Policies. This enables the automation of the audit process, making it more efficient and less prone to human error than manual inspection.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_conditional_access_policy_audit_device_code_flow

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Azure Resource/Service: Microsoft Entra ID (Azure Active Directory)
- API/CLI Method: Microsoft Graph API, Azure AD PowerShell module
- Properties to Check: 
  - `conditionalAccessPolicies` endpoint in Microsoft Graph API
  - Check `assignments.users` and `assignments.targetResources` for relevant policies
  - Verify `conditions.authenticationFlows` for device code flow settings
- Pass Criteria: Policies are configured to exclude unnecessary users and target resources, and device code flow is appropriately restricted.
- Fail Criteria: Policies include unnecessary users or target resources, or device code flow is not restricted as required.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The first AI opinion suggested a manual approach, while the second AI opinion highlighted the potential for automation. Given the availability of Azure APIs and PowerShell modules to manage and audit Conditional Access Policies, automation is feasible and preferable for this compliance control.
